parts.content: Define what is meant by a leaving group.
parts.markscheme:
A group that departs during a substitution, taking both bonding electrons
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction context
steps.content: In organic chemistry, particularly during **nucleophilic substitution** reactions ($S_N1$ or $S_N2$), a nucleophile attacks a substrate molecule and replaces an existing group. To understand what a leaving group is, we first need to look at which part of the molecule is being replaced.

highlights.text: Define what is meant by a leaving group.

steps.title: Analyze the bond breaking
steps.content:
When a leaving group departs, the covalent bond between it and the carbon atom must break. In these reactions, the bond undergoes **heterolytic fission**. 

This means that instead of each atom taking one electron, the leaving group departs and takes **both electrons** from the shared pair. This process usually results in the leaving group becoming a negatively charged ion (anion) or a neutral molecule.

steps.title: Formulate the final definition
steps.content:
Based on the standard chemical definition, we can define a leaving group as:

An atom or group of atoms that **departs** from a substrate molecule during a substitution reaction, taking **both electrons** from the covalent bond with it.

To earn the mark, you must mention both its departure and the fact that it retains the bonding pair of electrons.

highlights.text: A group that departs during a substitution, taking both bonding electrons

parts.content: Compare the rate of substitution for $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$ and $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2I}$ with the same nucleophile.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2I}$ reacts faster than $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the leaving groups
steps.content: The two molecules provided are chloroethane ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$) and iodoethane ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2I}$). In a nucleophilic substitution reaction, the halogen atom ($\mathrm{Cl}$ or $\mathrm{I}$) acts as the leaving group.

steps.title: Analyze the reactivity trend
steps.content:
In Group 17 of the periodic table, the reactivity of halogenoalkanes in substitution reactions increases as you move down the group:

$$\text{Fluoroalkane} < \text{Chloroalkane} < \text{Bromoalkane} < \text{Iodoalkane}$$

This trend occurs because the carbon–halogen bond strength decreases down the group (the $\mathrm{C-I}$ bond is much weaker and easier to break than the $\mathrm{C-Cl}$ bond). For more on periodic trends, you can review **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content: Since iodine is lower in Group 17 than chlorine, iodoethane is more reactive than chloroethane. Therefore, $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2I}$ will react at a faster rate than $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$.

steps.title: Identify the chemical bond change
steps.content: In a nucleophilic substitution reaction of a haloalkane, the key step that determines the reaction rate is the breaking of the carbon-halogen ($C-X$) bond. We are comparing chloroethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$) and iodoethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{I}$).

highlights.text: identity of the leaving group affects substitution reaction rates
Compare the rate of substitution for $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$ and $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2I}$

steps.title: Analyze bond enthalpy trends
steps.content:
According to **Topic R1.2: Energy cycles in reactions**, bond enthalpy is the energy required to break a bond. As we go down Group 17 from Chlorine to Iodine:
- The atomic radius of the halogen increases.
- The distance between the nuclei increases, making the $C-X$ bond longer.
- Longer bonds are generally weaker and have lower bond enthalpies.

Therefore, the bond enthalpy of the $C-I$ bond is lower than that of the $C-Cl$ bond.

steps.title: Relate enthalpy to rate
steps.content:
A lower bond enthalpy means the bond requires less energy to break. This effectively lowers the activation energy ($E_a$) for the substitution reaction. 

Because the $C-I$ bond is weaker than the $C-Cl$ bond, it breaks more easily during collisions with a nucleophile, leading to a faster reaction rate.

steps.title: State the final comparison
steps.content: Based on the comparison of bond enthalpies, $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{I}$ reacts faster than $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$.

parts.content: Explain your answer to part 2 with reference to bond strength.
parts.markscheme:
- The $\mathrm{C-I}$ bond is weaker than the $\mathrm{C-Cl}$ bond
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Weaker bond breaks more easily, increasing rate
<marks>1 mark</marks>

methods.label: Bond Strength and Ease of Cleavage

steps.title: Compare C-X bond strengths
steps.content:
To understand why reaction rates differ, we first look at the strength of the bond between the carbon atom and the leaving group. 

In the halogen group (Group 17), atomic radius increases as we go down the group. Because iodine is a much larger atom than chlorine, the $\text{C-I}$ bond is longer and significantly **weaker** than the $\text{C-Cl}$ bond. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.2: Energy cycles in reactions**, where we learn that bond enthalpy decreases as bond length increases.

steps.title: Relate strength to reaction rate
steps.content:
In any nucleophilic substitution reaction, the bond to the leaving group must be broken. 

Because the $\text{C-I}$ bond is weaker, it requires less energy to break during the reaction process. This means the bond is **cleaved more easily**, leading to a lower activation energy and a **faster reaction rate** compared to the stronger $\text{C-Cl}$ bond.

In an exam, remember that even though the $\text{C-Cl}$ bond is more polar, the **bond strength** is the dominant factor that determines the rate of these reactions.

steps.title: Compare bond strengths
steps.content:
In a nucleophilic substitution reaction, the bond between the carbon atom and the leaving group must be broken. The first step in explaining the difference in rate is to compare the bond enthalpies (strengths) of the two halogens involved.

The $\text{C-I}$ bond is weaker than the $\text{C-Cl}$ bond. This is because iodine has a much larger atomic radius than chlorine, resulting in a longer bond where the shared pair of electrons is further from the nuclei and thus less strongly attracted.

highlights.text: The C-I bond is weaker than the C-Cl bond

steps.title: Relate to activation energy
steps.content:
The strength of the bond being broken directly influences the **activation energy** ($E_a$) of the reaction. 

Because the $\text{C-I}$ bond is weaker, it requires less energy to reach the transition state where the bond is partially or fully broken. Consequently, the reaction involving the iodoalkane has a **lower activation energy** than the reaction involving the chloroalkane.

steps.title: Link $E_a$ to rate
steps.content:
According to collision theory, a lower activation energy means that at a given temperature, a larger proportion of colliding particles will have kinetic energy ($E \ge E_a$).

This leads to a higher frequency of successful collisions, which results in a faster reaction rate. This explains why iodoalkanes react faster than chloroalkanes, despite chlorine being more electronegative.

highlights.text: Weaker bond breaks more easily, increasing rate

parts.content: Write the equation for the reaction between $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ and $\mathrm{CN}^-$.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{CH_3Cl} + \mathrm{CN}^- \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CN} + \mathrm{Cl}^-$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content: The question asks for the reaction between chloromethane ($\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$) and the cyanide ion ($\mathrm{CN^-}$). This is a **nucleophilic substitution** reaction, a key concept covered in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Predict the substitution products
steps.content:
In a nucleophilic substitution, the nucleophile ($\mathrm{CN^-}$) attacks the partially positive carbon atom and replaces the leaving group (the chlorine atom). 

*   The $\mathrm{CN}$ group attaches to the methyl group to form **ethanenitrile** ($\mathrm{CH_3CN}$).
*   The chlorine atom leaves as a **chloride ion** ($\mathrm{Cl^-}$).

steps.title: Construct the balanced equation
steps.content:
Using the **Ionic Equation Approach**, we represent the nucleophile and the leaving group as ions. We place the reactants on the left and the products on the right:

$$\mathrm{CH_3Cl} + \mathrm{CN}^- \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CN} + \mathrm{Cl}^-$$

Notice that the equation is balanced both by mass (the number of each type of atom) and by charge (the total charge on both sides is $-1$).

parts.content: Identify the organic product.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{CH_3CN}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the nucleophile
steps.content:
In this reaction, we are looking at **nucleophilic substitution**. A nucleophile is an electron-rich species that donates an electron pair to an electron-deficient carbon. When reacting with cyanide salts (like $\text{KCN}$), the nucleophile is the cyanide ion:

$$\text{:CN}^-$$

steps.title: Identify the reactant
steps.content:
To produce the specific product $\text{CH}_3\text{CN}$, the starting material must be a halogenoalkane with one carbon atom, such as bromomethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Br}$) or chloromethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$).

In these molecules, the carbon-halogen bond is polar ($C^{\delta+}—X^{\delta-}$), making the carbon atom susceptible to attack by the nucleophile.

steps.title: Substitute the functional group
steps.content:
During the reaction, the $\text{CN}^-$ ion attacks the $\delta+$ carbon atom. The halogen atom (the leaving group) is displaced. 

We replace the halogen atom ($-X$) with the nitrile functional group ($-CN$):

$$\text{CH}_3X + \text{CN}^- \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CN} + X^-$$

steps.title: State the organic product
steps.content:
The resulting organic product contains a new carbon-carbon bond, increasing the chain length by one. The product is **ethanenitrile**.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CN}$$

parts.content: State the role of $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
It acts as an electrophile by accepting an electron pair.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content: The question specifies that halogenoalkanes like $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ (chloromethane) undergo **substitution reactions** with **nucleophiles**. In organic chemistry, reactions are often classified by how electron pairs move between the reacting species.

highlights.text: Halogenoalkanes can undergo substitution with different nucleophiles.

steps.title: Analyze the interaction
steps.content:
A nucleophile is an electron-rich species that donates an electron pair. In this reaction, the nucleophile attacks the carbon atom in $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ because the $\mathrm{C-Cl}$ bond is polar, leaving the carbon with a partial positive charge ($\delta+$).

Since $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ is the species being attacked and is receiving the electron pair from the nucleophile, it is acting as the electron-pair acceptor.

steps.title: Classify the role
steps.content:
By definition, a species that accepts an electron pair to form a new covalent bond is called an **electrophile**. 

According to the markscheme, you earn the mark by identifying both the term and the underlying reason: it acts as an electrophile because it accepts an electron pair.

steps.title: Analyze the bond polarity
steps.content:
To understand the role of $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$, we first look at the bond between carbon and chlorine. Chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, which creates a polar covalent bond. 

This means the carbon atom carries a partial positive charge ($͆+$), making it electron-deficient. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

steps.title: Link to electron pairs
steps.content: The question mentions that halogenoalkanes react with **nucleophiles**. By definition, a nucleophile is an electron-pair donor. In this reaction, the nucleophile donates its lone pair of electrons to the electron-deficient carbon atom in $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ to form a new bond.

steps.title: Identify as an electrophile
steps.content:
Since $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ (specifically the carbon atom) is the species that **accepts** the incoming electron pair from the nucleophile, it acts as an **electrophile**. 

According to the IB markscheme, the correct role to state is that it acts as an electrophile because it is an electron pair acceptor.

parts.content: Explain why $\mathrm{CN}^-$ is considered a strong nucleophile.
parts.markscheme:
It has a lone pair and a negative charge.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define nucleophile
steps.content: To understand why $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong nucleophile, we first need to recall what a nucleophile is. In **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**, we learn that a nucleophile is an electron-rich species that donates a pair of electrons to an electron-deficient center (an electrophile) to form a new covalent bond.

steps.title: Identify lone pair
steps.content: The first key structural feature is the presence of a **lone pair of electrons**. If we look at the Lewis structure of the cyanide ion, $[:\text{C} \equiv \text{N}:]^-$, there is a lone pair on the carbon atom. This lone pair is available to be donated to form a coordinate bond with an electrophile.

steps.title: Identify negative charge
steps.content: The second key feature is its **overall negative charge**. The cyanide ion is an anion, denoted by the $-$ sign in $\text{CN}^-$. This negative charge increases the electron density on the ion, making it much more attracted to the positive or partial positive charge $(\delta+)$ on an electrophile compared to a neutral molecule.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Combining these two features, $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong nucleophile because it possesses both a **lone pair of electrons** and a **negative charge**. 

In an IB exam, mentioning both of these specific structural characteristics is essential to earn the mark.

parts.content: Define heterolytic fission and explain how it differs from homolytic fission.
parts.markscheme:
- process where both bonding electrons from the covalent bond go to one of the atoms <marks>A1</marks>
- in homolytic fission, each of the two atoms receives one electron from the covalent bond <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define Heterolytic Fission
steps.content:
In chemistry, 'fission' refers to the breaking of a covalent bond. Following the **Electron Distribution Approach**, we define heterolytic fission by where the shared pair of electrons ends up.

**Heterolytic fission** is the process where a covalent bond breaks and **both electrons** from the bonding pair move to **one** of the atoms. 

This typically results in the formation of a positive ion (cation) and a negative ion (anion):  
$$X-Y \rightarrow X^+ + :Y^-$$

highlights.text: process where both bonding electrons from the covalent bond go to one of the atoms

steps.title: Compare with Homolytic Fission
steps.content:
To explain the difference, we look at how the electron distribution differs in homolytic fission. 

In **homolytic fission**, the covalent bond breaks evenly. Instead of the pair moving together, **one electron** from the bonding pair moves to **each** of the two atoms. 

This process results in the formation of two neutral, highly reactive species called **radicals**:  
$$X-Y \rightarrow X^. + Y^.$$

This distinction in how the electron pair is distributed is the fundamental difference between the two processes.

highlights.text: explain how it differs from homolytic fission
in homolytic fission, each of the two atoms receives one electron from the covalent bond

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks, ensure you clearly state the destination of the electrons for both types:

1.  **Heterolytic:** Both electrons $\rightarrow$ one atom (forming ions).
2.  **Homolytic:** One electron $\rightarrow$ each atom (forming radicals).

This topic is covered in **Reactivity 3.3 and 3.4** of your syllabus. Understanding these mechanisms is essential for predicting the outcomes of substitution and addition reactions.

parts.content: Write an equation for the heterolytic fission of $\text{CH}_3\text{I}$.
parts.markscheme: - balanced equation showing charge separation: $\text{CH}_3\text{I} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3^+ + \text{I}^-$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reactant
steps.content: The question asks for the equation of the heterolytic fission of $\text{CH}_3\text{I}$ (iodomethane). This concept is part of **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Define heterolytic fission
steps.content:
In heterolytic fission, when a covalent bond breaks, both electrons from the shared pair move to the same atom. This usually happens to the more electronegative atom in the bond. 

In the case of the $\text{C}-\text{I}$ bond, iodine is more electronegative than carbon, so it will take both electrons.

steps.title: Identify the ions formed
steps.content:
Because the iodine atom takes both electrons, it becomes a negatively charged ion (anion), and the carbon atom is left with a positive charge (cation):

- The $\text{CH}_3$ group becomes the methyl cation: $\text{CH}_3^+$
- The iodine atom becomes the iodide ion: $\text{I}^-$

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
Combining the reactant and the resulting ions, we get the direct ionic equation for the process:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{I} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3^+ + \text{I}^-$$

To earn the mark, ensure you show the full reactant on the left and both correctly charged species on the right.

parts.content: In the reaction $\text{CH}_3\text{I} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{I}^-$, draw the curly arrows to show attack by the nucleophile and departure of the leaving group.
parts.markscheme:
- curly arrow from a lone pair on the oxygen atom of $\text{OH}^-$ to the carbon atom in $\text{CH}_3\text{I}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- curly arrow from the $\text{C}-\text{I}$ bond to the iodine atom <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify roles in the reaction
steps.content:
In this nucleophilic substitution reaction, we first need to identify the electron-rich and electron-poor sites. The hydroxide ion ($\text{OH}^-$) has lone pairs on the oxygen atom, making it the **nucleophile**. In iodomethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{I}$), the iodine atom is more electronegative than the carbon atom, which creates a partial positive charge ($\delta^+$) on the carbon, making it the **electrophilic center**.

This reaction falls under **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Draw the nucleophilic attack
steps.content:
A curly arrow represents the movement of a pair of electrons. To show the attack, you must draw a curly arrow starting exactly from a lone pair on the oxygen atom of the $\text{OH}^-$ ion and pointing directly to the electrophilic carbon atom in $\text{CH}_3\text{I}$.

This represents the formation of a new $\text{C}-\text{O}$ bond.

highlights.text: curly arrow from a lone pair on the oxygen atom of $\text{OH}^-$ to the carbon atom in $\text{CH}_3\text{I}$

steps.title: Show the leaving group depart
steps.content:
As the new bond forms, the carbon atom would exceed its octet if it didn't lose a bond. The $\text{C}-\text{I}$ bond breaks via **heterolytic fission**. 

You must draw a second curly arrow starting from the middle of the $\text{C}-\text{I}$ bond and pointing toward the iodine atom. This shows that both electrons from the bond are moving onto the iodine to form an iodide ion ($\text{I}^-$).

highlights.text: curly arrow from the $\text{C}-\text{I}$ bond to the iodine atom

steps.title: Final mechanistic representation
steps.content:
Combining these two steps gives you the full mechanism for the reaction:

$$\text{HO}:^- \curvearrowright \text{CH}_3{-}\text{I} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{I}^-$$

(Where the second arrow starts at the bond between C and I). Following these steps ensures you earn both marks by showing exactly where the electrons start and where they end up.

steps.title: Analyze the requirement
steps.content: The question asks for a comparison between the $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ and $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ mechanisms, specifically focusing on the **number of steps** involved in each. This is a fundamental concept in organic chemistry found in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**.

highlights.text: Compare the mechanisms of $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ and $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ reactions with reference to the number of steps involved.

steps.title: Explain the SN1 mechanism
steps.content:
The $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ (Substitution Nucleophilic Unimolecular) mechanism is a **two-step process**. 

1. The first step involves the departure of the leaving group to form a **carbocation intermediate**.
2. The second step involves the nucleophile attacking this carbocation to form the final product.

Stating both the number of steps and the presence of the intermediate is essential for the first mark.

steps.title: Explain the SN2 mechanism
steps.content:
The $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ (Substitution Nucleophilic Bimolecular) mechanism is a **one-step, concerted process**. 

In this mechanism, the nucleophile attacks the carbon atom at the exact same time the leaving group leaves. Because these events happen simultaneously, there is no intermediate formed—only a single transition state. Mentioning it is "one-step" or "concerted" earns the second mark.

steps.title: Final Comparison Summary
steps.content:
To summarize the comparison for the examiner:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text{Mechanism} & \text{Description of Steps} \\ \hline \text{S}_{\text{N}}1 & \text{Two-step process with a carbocation intermediate} \\ \hline \text{S}_{\text{N}}2 & \text{One-step, concerted process} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

steps.title: Define the mechanisms
steps.content: To compare these mechanisms, we look at how the reaction proceeds from reactants to products. The names $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ and $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ give us a hint about their molecularity, which determines the structure of their energy profiles and the number of steps involved.

highlights.text: Compare the mechanisms of SN1 and SN2 reactions with reference to the number of steps involved.

steps.title: Analyze SN1 energy profile
steps.content:
The $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism is a **two-step** process. 

In an energy profile diagram, this is represented by **two activation energy peaks**. 
1. The first peak is the slow, rate-determining step where the bond to the leaving group breaks.
2. The 'valley' between the peaks represents the formation of a temporary **carbocation intermediate**.
3. The second peak is the fast step where the nucleophile attacks the carbocation.

steps.title: Analyze SN2 energy profile
steps.content:
The $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ mechanism is a **one-step**, concerted process. 

Because everything happens at the same time (the nucleophile attacks while the leaving group departs), the energy profile shows only **one activation energy peak**. This single peak represents a single **transition state** where the carbon is momentarily bonded to both the nucleophile and the leaving group. There is no intermediate formed in this mechanism.

steps.title: Final Comparison
steps.content:
In summary, to earn the marks on an IB exam:

*   **$\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$**: A **two-step** mechanism that involves a carbocation intermediate.
*   **$\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$**: A **one-step** (concerted) mechanism with no intermediates.

highlights.text: SN1: Two-step mechanism involving a carbocation intermediate

parts.content: For the reaction of $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CBr}$ with $\text{OH}^-$, identify the likely mechanism and explain your choice.
parts.markscheme:

- Explain choice: Tertiary halogenoalkane stabilizes the carbocation intermediate <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the molecular structure
steps.content:
First, we look at the structure of the reactant, $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CBr}$. In this molecule, the carbon atom bonded to the bromine (the halogen) is directly attached to three methyl ($-\text{CH}_3$) groups.

According to the classification rules in **Topic S3.2 (Functional Groups)**, this makes it a **tertiary halogenoalkane**. Identifying the degree of substitution is the essential first step in predicting the reaction pathway.

steps.title: Identify the mechanism
steps.content:
Based on the structural analysis, tertiary halogenoalkanes react almost exclusively via the **$\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism** (Unimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution).

Unlike primary halogenoalkanes which favor $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$, the bulky groups surrounding the central carbon in a tertiary molecule prevent a nucleophile like $\text{OH}^-$ from attacking directly in a single step.

steps.title: Justify via carbocation stability
steps.content:
The choice of the $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism is justified by the stability of the intermediate formed. In the first step, the $\text{C}-\text{Br}$ bond breaks heterolytically to form a **tertiary carbocation**, $((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+)$.

As covered in **Topic R3.4 (Electron-pair sharing reactions)**, tertiary carbocations are highly stable due to the **positive inductive effect**. The three methyl groups push electron density toward the positive carbon center, spreading out the charge and lowering the energy of the intermediate.

highlights.text: Explain choice: Tertiary halogenoalkane stabilizes the carbocation intermediate

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, you must explicitly state both the mechanism and the reason:

1.  **Mechanism:** $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$
2.  **Reason:** It is a tertiary halogenoalkane which forms a stable tertiary carbocation intermediate.

- Nucleophilic attack: Curly arrow from lone pair on $\text{OH}^-$ to the carbocation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Formation of product: $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{COH}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Select a Tertiary Halogenoalkane
steps.content:
To outline an $S_N1$ mechanism, we typically use a tertiary halogenoalkane because it forms a stable carbocation intermediate. Let's use **2-bromo-2-methylpropane**, $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CBr}$, reacting with a hydroxide ion, $\text{OH}^-$.

This process is covered in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**, specifically under $S_N1$ mechanisms (R3.4.9).

steps.title: Step 1: Heterolytic Fission
steps.content:
The first step is the slow, rate-determining step. The bond between the carbon and bromine breaks heterolytically. 

**Action:** Draw a curly arrow starting from the center of the $\text{C}-\text{Br}$ bond pointing directly to the $\text{Br}$ atom. This shows both electrons from the bond moving to the bromine.

$$\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{C}—\text{Br} \rightarrow \text{(CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+ + \text{Br}^-$$

highlights.text: Curly arrow from C–Br bond to Br atom, forming (CH3)3C+ and Br-

steps.title: Step 2: Nucleophilic Attack
steps.content:
The intermediate is a tertiary carbocation, $\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+$. Because it is tertiary, it is stabilized by the positive inductive effect of the three methyl groups (Topic R3.4.12). Now, the nucleophile ($\text{OH}^-$) attacks this electron-deficient carbon.

**Action:** Draw a curly arrow starting from a lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom of the $\text{OH}^-$ ion, pointing to the positive carbon atom ($C^+$).

$$\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+ + {:\text{OH}}^- \rightarrow \text{(CH}_3)_3\text{COH}$$

highlights.text: Curly arrow from lone pair on OH– to the carbocation

steps.title: Step 3: State Final Product
steps.content:
The final product of this substitution reaction is a tertiary alcohol. 

**Product:** **2-methylpropan-2-ol**, $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{COH}$.

In an exam, ensuring your curly arrows start at a bond or a lone pair and end exactly on an atom is crucial for gaining full marks!

steps.title: Identify the reaction context
steps.content: In organic chemistry, specifically within **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**, we study how molecules like halogenoalkanes react. When a nucleophile attacks a halogenoalkane, the bond between the carbon and the halogen breaks, and the halogen is displaced. This displaced part is known as the leaving group.

highlights.text: Leaving groups affect the reactivity of halogenoalkanes.

steps.title: Define the leaving group
steps.content:
The formal definition of a **leaving group** is an atom or group of atoms that departs from a molecule with **both electrons** from the shared covalent bond during a reaction (such as nucleophilic substitution).

This process is an example of **heterolytic fission** ($R3.4.3$), where the bond breaks unevenly and the leaving group retains the electron pair to become an anion (like $Cl^-$, $Br^-$, or $I^-$).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, your definition should specify both the departure and the fate of the electrons:

**A leaving group is an atom or group that departs with both bonding electrons during a substitution reaction.**

Hi there! To find the leaving group, we first need to look at the overall reaction provided in the question. This is a nucleophilic substitution reaction, which is a key concept in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**.

$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br} + \mathrm{OH}^- \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH} + \mathrm{Br}^-$$

steps.title: Track the organic substrate
steps.content:
Look at the organic molecule (the substrate) before and after the reaction. 

- **Reactant:** $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br}$ (bromoethane)
- **Product:** $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$ (ethanol)

We can see that the bromine atom ($\mathrm{Br}$) in the reactant has been replaced by the hydroxide group ($-\mathrm{OH}$) in the product.

steps.title: Identify the displaced group
steps.content:
By definition, the **leaving group** is the species that is displaced from the carbon atom and takes a pair of electrons with it. 

In this equation, the bromine atom leaves the organic chain and appears on the product side as an independent species: the bromide ion, $\mathrm{Br}^-$.

steps.title: State the final answer

Based on our identification from the equation, the leaving group is the bromide ion.

steps.title: Identify Group 17 elements
steps.content:
To find a suitable leaving group for a halogenoalkane, we look at **Group 17** of the periodic table (the halogens). The elements in this group include:

*   Fluorine (F)
*   Chlorine (Cl)
*   Bromine (Br)
*   Iodine (I)

In nucleophilic substitution reactions, the halogen atom leaves the molecule as a negatively charged **halide ion**.

steps.title: Select an alternative halide
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Identification** approach, we can choose any halogen from Group 17 (besides the one already mentioned or implied) and name its corresponding ion. 

Two common examples that act as excellent leaving groups are:
1.  **Chloride** ($$\text{Cl}^-$$)
2.  **Iodide** ($$\text{I}^-$$)

Either of these is a valid answer for this question.

steps.title: Identify reactants and states
steps.content:
First, we identify the chemical formulas and physical states for the starting materials. Bromoethane is an organic liquid, and the question specifies that the hydroxide is in an aqueous environment.

*   **Bromoethane:** $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br(l)}$
*   **Aqueous hydroxide:** $\mathrm{OH^-(aq)}$

This reaction falls under **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions** in your IB syllabus, specifically focusing on nucleophilic substitution.

steps.title: Determine the substitution products
steps.content:
Using the **Ionic Substitution Approach**, we recognize that the hydroxide ion ($\mathrm{OH^-}$) acts as a nucleophile. It targets the partially positive carbon in bromoethane, displacing the bromine atom. 

1.  The bromine atom leaves as a **bromide ion**: $\mathrm{Br^-(aq)}$.
2.  The hydroxide group replaces it to form **ethanol**: $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH(aq)}$.

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
Finally, we combine the reactants and products into a single equation. We must ensure that both the mass (number of atoms) and the net charge are balanced. Since one $\mathrm{OH^-}$ replaces one $\mathrm{Br}$ atom, the stoichiometry is 1:1.

$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br(l)} + \mathrm{OH^-(aq)} \to \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH(aq)} + \mathrm{Br^-(aq)}$$

To earn the mark, make sure you include all **state symbols** as requested.

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
Bromoethane ($CH_3CH_2Br$) is a **primary haloalkane**. Because the carbon attached to the bromine is only attached to one other carbon, it is relatively unhindered. This allows a nucleophile like the hydroxide ion ($OH^-$) to attack it directly in a single, concerted step. This is known as an $S_N2$ mechanism (Substitution Nucleophilic Bimolecular).

This concept is covered in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Identify bond polarity
steps.content:
Before drawing the arrows, we must identify the partial charges. Bromine is more electronegative than carbon, creating a polar bond:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2^{\delta+} \text{--- Br}^{\delta-}$$

The carbon atom ($C$) is **electrophilic** (electron-seeking) because of its partial positive charge, making it the target for the nucleophile.

steps.title: Draw the nucleophilic attack
steps.content:
The first mark in the markscheme is awarded for showing the start of the reaction. Draw a curly arrow starting from a **lone pair** on the oxygen of the hydroxide ion ($OH^-$) pointing directly to the electrophilic carbon atom in bromoethane.

$$\text{HO}:^- \curvearrowright \text{C}(\text{H}_2)(\text{CH}_3)\text{---Br}$$

highlights.text: Curly arrow from lone pair on OH^- to electrophilic carbon in CH_3CH_2Br

steps.title: Show the leaving group
steps.content: As the $C-O$ bond begins to form, the $C-Br$ bond must break because carbon can only form four bonds. The second mark is for a curly arrow starting from the center of the **C–Br bond** and pointing to the **bromine atom**. This indicates that the electron pair from the bond is moving onto the bromine to form a bromide ion ($Br^-$).

highlights.text: Curly arrow from C–Br bond to Br, showing departure of leaving group

steps.title: Final $S_N2$ mechanism
steps.content:
To earn the final mark, ensure both arrows are shown simultaneously on the same diagram to represent a **one-step concerted process**. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{HO}:^- + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br} &\rightarrow [\text{HO}\cdots\text{CH}_2(\text{CH}_3)\cdots\text{Br}]^{\ddagger} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{Br}^- \end{aligned}$$

In your drawing, ensure the arrows are precisely placed: one from the $OH^-$ lone pair to the $C$, and one from the $C-Br$ bond to the $Br$.

highlights.text: mechanism correctly shown as one-step S_N2

steps.title: Analyze the reaction ratio
steps.content:
In a nucleophilic substitution reaction, one mole of bromoehtane ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br}$) reacts with hydroxide to produce one mole of ethanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$). 

This is a $1:1$ stoichiometric ratio, which means the number of moles of ethanol produced will be equal to the number of moles of bromoethane reacted, assuming a $100\%$ yield.

steps.title: Find bromoethane's molar mass
steps.content:
First, we calculate the molar mass ($M$) of bromoethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Br}$) by summing the relative atomic masses from the periodic table:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Br}) &= (2 \times 12.01) + (5 \times 1.01) + 79.90 \\ &= 108.97 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum of atomic masses on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of atomic masses directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the sum of atomic masses into the expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of bromoethane.

steps.title: Calculate moles of bromoethane
steps.content:
Using the formula for the number of moles from the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet, we convert the given mass into moles:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{bromoethane}) &= \frac{3.00 \text{ g}}{108.97 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ &\approx 0.0275 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Divide mass by molar mass to find moles.

steps.title: Find ethanol's molar mass
steps.content:
To eventually find the mass of ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$), we need its molar mass as well:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}) &= (2 \times 12.01) + (6 \times 1.01) + 16.00 \\ &= 46.08 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Sum the atomic masses for C2H6O.

ti84.instructions: Sum the atomic masses: 2 carbons, 6 hydrogens, and 1 oxygen.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of ethanol.

steps.title: Calculate theoretical mass
steps.content:
Since the ratio is $1:1$, we have $0.0275 \text{ mol}$ of ethanol. Now, we rearrange the mole formula to solve for mass ($m = n \times M$):

$$\begin{aligned} m(\text{ethanol}) &= 0.0275 \text{ mol} \times 46.08 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \\ &= 1.27 \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

This is the theoretical mass produced at $100\%$ yield. This problem uses stoichiometry concepts covered in **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous mole result by 46.08.

calculator.description: Multiply moles by molar mass to find the final mass.

steps.title: Identify the reaction stoichiometry
steps.content:
First, we look at the balanced equation for the nucleophilic substitution. One molecule of bromoethane ($ \mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br} $) reacts with one hydroxide ion to produce one molecule of ethanol ($ \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH} $). 

This means the molar ratio is $1:1$. In terms of mass, this implies that one molar mass of bromoethane will produce exactly one molar mass of ethanol.

steps.title: Calculate reactant molar mass
steps.content:
Using the relative atomic masses from the periodic table, we calculate the molar mass ($M$) for bromoethane ($ \mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br} $):

$$\begin{aligned} M(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br}) &= (2 \times 12.01) + (5 \times 1.01) + 79.90 \\ &= 24.02 + 5.05 + 79.90 \\ &= 108.97 \mathrm{~g~mol^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

This is a key step that earns **1 mark** in the markscheme.

calculator.description: Verify the molar mass of bromoethane.

steps.title: Calculate product molar mass
steps.content:
Next, we find the molar mass for the product, ethanol ($ \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH} $):

$$\begin{aligned} M(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}) &= (2 \times 12.01) + (6 \times 1.01) + 16.00 \\ &= 24.02 + 6.06 + 16.00 \\ &= 46.08 \mathrm{~g~mol^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Verify the molar mass of ethanol.

steps.title: Apply the mass ratio
steps.content:
Now we use the **Mass Ratio Method** (unitary method). We know that $108.97\mathrm{~g}$ of bromoethane produces $46.08\mathrm{~g}$ of ethanol.

To find how much ethanol is produced from $3.00\mathrm{~g}$ of bromoethane, we set up the proportion:

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 3.00\mathrm{~g} \times \frac{46.08\mathrm{~g}}{108.97\mathrm{~g}}$$

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} \approx 1.27\mathrm{~g}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get $1.27\mathrm{~g}$. This completes the final marks for the calculation.

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content: In this reaction, we are reacting bromomethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Br}$) with ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$). This is a nucleophilic substitution reaction, where ammonia acts as the nucleophile due to the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom.

steps.title: Formation of the salt
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Salt Formation** approach, the nitrogen from the ammonia attacks the electrophilic carbon in the methyl group. This causes the $\text{C}-\text{Br}$ bond to break heterolytically. 

Because the ammonia molecule was neutral, when it forms a new bond with the methyl group, the resulting nitrogen atom becomes positively charged. This creates the methylammonium cation and a bromide anion:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{Br} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_3^+ + \text{Br}^-$$

steps.title: Final chemical equation
steps.content:
The final equation representing the formation of the salt is:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{Br} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_3^+ + \text{Br}^-$$

Note that in many contexts, this salt (methylammonium bromide) might react further with excess ammonia to form the neutral amine, but the direct salt formation stops at this ionic stage.

steps.title: Identify the nucleophilic substitution
steps.content: In this reaction, bromomethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Br}$) reacts with ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$). Because nitrogen has a lone pair of electrons, ammonia acts as a **neutral nucleophile**. This process is a nucleophilic substitution, which you can find more about in **Topic R3.4: Nucleophilic Substitution reactions**.

steps.title: Form the intermediate salt
steps.content:
The first step of the reaction involves the ammonia molecule attacking the carbon atom in $\text{CH}_3\text{Br}$. This results in the formation of an alkylammonium salt. According to the markscheme, this equation is the required answer for the mark:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{Br} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_3^+ + \text{Br}^-$$

steps.title: Neutralize with excess ammonia
steps.content:
Using the **Complete Reaction to Primary Amine** approach, we recognize that in the presence of excess ammonia, the intermediate methylammonium ion ($\text{CH}_3\text{NH}_3^+$) is neutralized. A second mole of ammonia acts as a base to remove a proton ($H^+$):

$$\text{CH}_3\text{NH}_3^+ + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2 + \text{NH}_4^+$$

steps.title: Write the overall equation
steps.content:
By combining the formation of the salt and the neutralization step, we get the overall balanced equation for the production of the primary amine (methylamine) and the byproduct ammonium bromide:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{Br} + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2 + \text{NH}_4\text{Br}$$

This equation accounts for the "complete reaction" using excess ammonia as specified in this method.

steps.title: Define the nucleophile
steps.content:
According to the Lewis definition, a nucleophile is an electron-rich species that **donates a lone pair of electrons** to an electron-deficient atom (an electrophile).

To be a nucleophile, the species must have at least one lone pair of electrons available to share. Common examples include the hydroxide ion ($OH^-$), water ($H_2O$), and ammonia ($NH_3$).

steps.title: Identify the bond formed
steps.content:
When the nucleophile donates its lone pair, it forms a **coordinate covalent bond** with the target atom. Because it acts as an electron-pair donor, a nucleophile is also classified as a **Lewis base**.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions** in your IB syllabus. You'll see this behavior frequently in nucleophilic substitution reactions ($S_N1$ and $S_N2$).

steps.title: Analyze the term
steps.content:
To understand what a nucleophile is, we can start by looking at the word itself. It comes from the Greek words **"nucleo"** (meaning nucleus) and **"phile"** (meaning loving). 

So, literally, a nucleophile is a "nucleus-loving" species. Since the nucleus of an atom is positively charged, this tells us that nucleophiles are attracted to positive centers.

steps.title: Understand the attraction
steps.content:
Because nucleophiles are "nucleus-loving," they are naturally attracted to regions of **positive charge** or **electron-deficient sites** (often represented as $\delta+$) in other molecules. 

For a species to be attracted to a positive center, it must be electron-rich itself. This attraction is the driving force behind their role in chemical reactions, which you can study further in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Define an electrophile
steps.content: In organic chemistry, an **electrophile** is a species that is "electron-loving." It is an electron-deficient species that can accept a pair of electrons from a nucleophile to form a new covalent bond. To identify the electrophile in this reaction, we need to find the molecule that has an electron-poor center.

steps.title: Analyze C-Cl bond polarity
steps.content:
Let's look at the $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ molecule. Within the $\text{C--Cl}$ bond, there is a significant difference in electronegativity. Chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, which means it pulls the shared pair of electrons toward itself.

This creates a polar bond, which we represent with partial charges:
$$\overset{\delta+}{\mathrm{C}}\text{---}\overset{\delta-}{\mathrm{Cl}}$$

Concepts of bond polarity are covered in **Topic S2.2.5: Bond Polarity**.

steps.title: Identify the electron-deficient atom
steps.content:
Because the carbon atom in $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ carries a partial positive charge ($͂+$), it is **electron-deficient**. This makes the carbon atom susceptible to attack by an electron-rich species (the nucleophile, $\mathrm{OH}^-$).

Since the carbon atom in $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ acts as the electron pair acceptor in this mechanism, the entire molecule is classified as the electrophile.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of the bond polarity and the resulting electron deficiency at the carbon atom, we conclude that the electrophile is chloromethane.

**Answer:** $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$

steps.title: Analyze the chemical equation
steps.content:
The reaction provided is a substitution reaction where one group replaces another. We are given the following equation:

$$\mathrm{CH_3Cl} + \mathrm{OH}^- \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3OH} + \mathrm{Cl}^-$$

steps.title: Identify the nucleophile
steps.content:
To find the electrophile, it is often easiest to identify the nucleophile first. 

A **nucleophile** is a "nucleus-loving" species. These are electron-rich species that have a lone pair of electrons (and often a negative charge) which they can donate to an electron-poor center. 

Looking at the reactants, the hydroxide ion ($$\mathrm{OH}^-$$) has a negative charge and multiple lone pairs on the oxygen atom, making it a clear nucleophile.

steps.title: Deduce the electrophile
steps.content:
In a nucleophilic substitution reaction, the nucleophile attacks the **electrophile**. 

An electrophile is an "electron-loving" species. These are electron-poor species (carrying a positive or partial positive charge) that accept an electron pair. 

Since we identified $$\mathrm{OH}^-$$ as the nucleophile, the other reactant, **chloromethane ($$\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$$)**, must be the electrophile. In this molecule, the highly electronegative chlorine atom pulls electron density away from the carbon, giving the carbon atom a partial positive charge ($$\delta+$$) that attracts the nucleophile.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: Based on our identification of the reactants' roles, the electrophile is $$\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$$.

steps.title: Define an electrophile
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, we define an **electrophile** (literally "electron-lover") as a species that is attracted to electron-rich regions. From a functional perspective, an electrophile is a chemical species that **accepts a pair of electrons** from another species to form a new covalent bond.

You can review these definitions in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Identify the carbon center
steps.content: In $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ (chloromethane), the carbon atom is bonded to a chlorine atom. Since chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, the $\mathrm{C-Cl}$ bond is polar. This leaves the carbon atom with a partial positive charge ($\delta+$), making it the site that is ready to interact with electrons.

steps.title: Explain the electron acceptance
steps.content:
Because that carbon atom is electron-deficient, it can **accept a lone pair of electrons** from a nucleophile. For example, in a substitution reaction, a hydroxide ion ($\mathrm{OH}^-$) acts as a nucleophile and donates its electron pair to the carbon atom in $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$. 

This ability to accept an electron pair is exactly why we classify the carbon in $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ as an electrophile.

In $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$, there is a covalent bond between the carbon ($\mathrm{C}$) and the chlorine ($\mathrm{Cl}$) atom. Chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, meaning it has a stronger pull on the shared pair of electrons.

parts.content: Write the balanced equation for the reaction between chloroethane and excess ammonia.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl + 2NH_3 \to CH_3CH_2NH_2 + NH_4Cl}$
<marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
In this scenario, chloroethane reacts with ammonia in a **nucleophilic substitution** reaction. The nitrogen atom in ammonia has a lone pair of electrons, making it a nucleophile that attacks the electron-deficient carbon atom bonded to the chlorine in chloroethane.

This reaction is covered in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions** of the IB syllabus.

steps.title: The role of first ammonia
steps.content:
Initially, one molecule of ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) replaces the chlorine atom to form the primary amine (ethanamine) and hydrogen chloride ($\text{HCl}$) as a byproduct:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 + \text{HCl}$$

steps.title: Neutralize with excess ammonia
steps.content:
The question specifies that we have **excess ammonia**. Ammonia is a base, so it immediately reacts with the acidic $\text{HCl}$ byproduct to form the salt, ammonium chloride ($\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$):

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

steps.title: State the balanced equation
steps.content:
By combining the two steps, we see that a total of **two moles** of ammonia are required for every one mole of chloroethane. This results in the formation of the primary amine and the ammonium salt.

The final balanced equation is:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + 2\text{NH}_3 \to \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

steps.title: Identify the primary substitution reaction
steps.content:
In the first stage of this process, the ammonia molecule acts as a nucleophile. It attacks the electron-deficient carbon atom in the chloroethane, displacing the chloride ion. This is an example of a **nucleophilic substitution reaction**, specifically covered in **Topic R3.4.2: Nucleophilic substitution reactions**.

$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl + NH_3 \to CH_3CH_2NH_2 + HCl}$$

steps.title: Account for excess ammonia
steps.content:
The question specifies that we have **excess ammonia**. The hydrogen chloride ($HCl$) produced in the first step is a strong acid, while ammonia ($NH_3$) is a weak base. They will react immediately in a neutralization reaction to form the salt, ammonium chloride.

$$\mathrm{NH_3 + HCl \to NH_4Cl}$$

steps.title: Combine the reactions
steps.content:
To find the overall balanced equation, we add the two steps together and cancel out the species that appear on both sides (the intermediate $HCl$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & \mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl + NH_3 \to CH_3CH_2NH_2 + HCl} \\ \text{Step 2: } & \mathrm{NH_3 + HCl \to NH_4Cl} \\ \hline \text{Overall: } & \mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl + 2NH_3 \to CH_3CH_2NH_2 + NH_4Cl} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final balanced equation
steps.content:
By combining the nucleophilic attack and the subsequent neutralization, we arrive at the final balanced equation required for the mark.

$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl + 2NH_3 \to CH_3CH_2NH_2 + NH_4Cl}$$

parts.content: Identify the organic and inorganic products formed in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Organic: ethylamine ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2NH_2}$)
<marks>1</marks>
Inorganic: ammonium chloride ($\mathrm{NH_4Cl}$)
<marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
In this reaction, we are reacting a **halogenoalkane** (chloroethane) with **excess ammonia**. This is a classic **nucleophilic substitution** reaction (specifically $S_N2$ for this primary halogenoalkane). Ammonia acts as a nucleophile, replacing the chlorine atom on the ethyl chain.

This concept is part of **Topic R3.4: Nucleophilic substitution reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Determine the organic product
steps.content:
When the nitrogen in ammonia ($\mathrm{NH_3}$) bonds to the carbon in chloroethane, it initially forms an intermediate cation. Because the reaction uses **excess ammonia**, a second ammonia molecule acts as a base to remove a proton ($H^+$), resulting in a stable **primary amine**.

The organic product is **ethylamine** (or aminoethane):

$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2NH_2}$$

steps.title: Determine the inorganic product
steps.content:
The chlorine atom leaves as a chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl^-}$). Meanwhile, the excess ammonia reacts with the $H^+$ ion removed during the formation of the amine to form an ammonium ion ($\mathrm{NH_4^+}$).

Together, these form the inorganic salt, **ammonium chloride**:

$$\mathrm{NH_4Cl}$$

The full balanced equation for this reaction is:
$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl} + 2\mathrm{NH_3} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CH_2NH_2} + \mathrm{NH_4Cl}$$

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, ensure you clearly state both products:

*   **Organic product:** Ethylamine ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2NH_2}$)
*   **Inorganic product:** Ammonium chloride ($\mathrm{NH_4Cl}$)

steps.title: Identify the nucleophile
steps.content: Ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) acts as a nucleophile because of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom. It reacts with **chloroethane**, which has a polar $\text{C}—\text{Cl}$ bond, making the carbon atom electrophilic ($\text{C}^{\delta+}$).

steps.title: Analyze the nucleophilic attack
steps.content:
In the $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ mechanism (covered in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**), the nitrogen lone pair attacks the electrophilic carbon. The chlorine atom is displaced as a leaving group ($\text{Cl}^-$). This creates an intermediate ethylammonium ion:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow [\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_3]^+ + \text{Cl}^-$$

steps.title: Deprotonation by excess ammonia
steps.content:
The question specifies **excess ammonia**. In this environment, a second $\text{NH}_3$ molecule acts as a Brønsted–Lowry base to remove a proton ($\text{H}^+$) from the intermediate ion, producing a stable **primary amine**:

$$\begin{aligned} [\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_3]^+ + \text{NH}_3 &\rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 + \text{NH}_4^+ \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final products
steps.content:
The primary amine produced is the organic product. The ammonium ion ($\text{NH}_4^+$) and the chloride ion ($\text{Cl}^-$) released earlier combine to form the inorganic product.

Organic: **ethylamine** ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2$)
Inorganic: **ammonium chloride** ($\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$)

steps.title: Identify the nucleophile and electrophile
steps.content:
In this reaction between chloroethane ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$) and ammonia ($\mathrm{NH_3}$), we first identify the reactive centers. The carbon atom bonded to the chlorine atom in chloroethane is **electrophilic** because the more electronegative chlorine atom pulls electron density away from it, creating a partial positive charge ($\delta+$).

Ammonia acts as a **nucleophile** because the nitrogen atom possesses a non-bonding **lone pair of electrons**, making it an electron-rich species attracted to electron-deficient centers.

steps.title: Describe the electron movement
steps.content:
The mechanism begins when the nitrogen atom in ammonia uses its **lone pair** to attack the **electrophilic carbon** atom in chloroethane. This movement of an electron pair from the nucleophile to the electrophile is what initiates the formation of a new chemical bond.

This step earns the first mark by correctly identifying the source of the electrons and their target.

highlights.text: Ammonia uses a lone pair on nitrogen to attack the electrophilic carbon in $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$

steps.title: Identify the bond type
steps.content:
As the lone pair from the nitrogen is shared with the carbon atom, a new C-N bond is formed. Since both electrons in this new covalent bond originated from the nitrogen atom (the nucleophile), it is specifically classified as a **dative covalent bond** (also known as a **coordinate bond**).

This identification earns the second mark for describing the nature of the bond formed during the initial nucleophilic attack.

highlights.text: Forms a dative covalent (coordinate) bond with carbon

steps.title: Identify the nucleophile
steps.content:
In this reaction, ammonia ($\mathrm{NH_3}$) acts as a **nucleophile**. A nucleophile is an electron-rich species that donates an electron pair to an electron-deficient center. Ammonia is able to do this because the nitrogen atom possesses a **lone pair** of electrons.

This concept is covered in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**, specifically under the definition of nucleophiles.

highlights.text: Ammonia uses a lone pair on nitrogen

steps.title: Locate the electrophilic center
steps.content:
Chloroethane ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$) contains a polar $\mathrm{C-Cl}$ bond. Because chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, it pulls electron density away, leaving the carbon atom with a partial positive charge ($\delta+$).

This electron-deficient carbon is known as the **electrophilic center**. In a mechanism diagram, we label this carbon as the target for the nucleophilic attack.

steps.title: Visualize electron movement
steps.content:
To describe the electron movement, we use a **curly arrow**. The arrow starts at the lone pair on the nitrogen atom and points directly to the electrophilic carbon atom. 

$$\mathrm{H_3N: \longrightarrow C^{\delta+}(H_2)-CH_3}$$

This arrow represents the movement of the electron pair to form a new chemical bond. This is the first mark-earning point in your exam response.

steps.title: Define the bond formed
steps.content:
When the nitrogen lone pair is shared with the carbon atom, a specific type of covalent bond is formed. Since both electrons in the shared pair originate from the nitrogen atom, it is called a **coordinate bond** (also known as a **dative covalent bond**).

You can review the properties of these bonds in **Topic S2.2.3: Coordination bonds**.

highlights.text: Forms a dative covalent (coordinate) bond with carbon

steps.title: Identify the Lewis roles
steps.content: In the Lewis acid-base model, we define species based on their ability to donate or accept electron pairs. Here, **ammonia** acts as the **Lewis base** because the nitrogen atom possesses a non-bonding (lone) pair of electrons. The carbon atom in the $\text{C--Cl}$ bond of chloroethane acts as the **Lewis acid** (or electrophile) because it is electron-deficient ($ \delta+ $) due to the electronegativity of chlorine.

steps.title: Describe electron movement
steps.content: The interaction begins when ammonia uses the lone pair of electrons on its nitrogen atom to "attack" the electrophilic carbon atom in chloroethane. In this process, the nitrogen atom **donates an electron pair** to the carbon atom, which acts as the **electron pair acceptor**.

steps.title: Define the bond formed
steps.content: As a result of this electron pair donation, a new bond is created between the nitrogen and the carbon. Since both electrons in this shared pair originate from the nitrogen atom, the bond formed is called a **coordinate bond** (also known as a **dative covalent bond**).

highlights.text: Forms a dative covalent (coordinate) bond with carbon

steps.title: Define an electrophile
steps.content:
To answer this, we first need to recall what an electrophile is. In the context of **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**, an electrophile is defined as an "electron-loving" species. 

These species are electron-deficient (often carrying a positive or partial positive charge) and are attracted to regions of high electron density, such as the double bond in an alkene.

steps.title: Analyze alkene reactivity
steps.content: Alkenes are characterized by their carbon-carbon double bond ($C=C$). This double bond contains a $\pi$-bond, which is a region of high electron density located above and below the plane of the molecule. This makes alkenes very attractive to electrophiles that are looking for electrons to form a new bond.

highlights.text: Electrophilic addition reactions are characteristic of alkenes.

steps.title: Identify the electrophilic reagent
steps.content:
In a typical electrophilic addition reaction, a common reagent used is hydrogen bromide ($HBr$). 

Because bromine is more electronegative than hydrogen, the $H-Br$ bond is polar. We can represent this as:

$$\overset{\delta+}{H}-\overset{\delta-}{Br}$$

The electron-deficient $H^{\delta+}$ atom acts as the electrophilic center that attacks the electron-rich double bond of the alkene. Therefore, the complete molecular reagent identified as the electrophile is $HBr$.

steps.title: Final identification
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, you can identify the electrophile as the whole molecule or the specific attacking part. Following the **Identify the Electrophilic Reagent** approach, we name the primary reagent involved.

Final Answer: $\text{HBr}$

steps.title: Define the electrophile
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, an **electrophile** is an electron-deficient species that is attracted to regions of high electron density. Since alkenes contain a carbon-carbon double bond ($\\text{C}=\\text{C}$) with a high density of $\\pi$ electrons, they are naturally attacked by these "electron-lovers."

This concept is covered in **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Analyze the reagent polarity
steps.content:
Consider a common reagent for this reaction, such as hydrogen bromide ($$HBr$$). Because bromine is more electronegative than hydrogen, the electrons in the $\\text{H}-\\text{Br}$ bond are pulled toward the bromine atom. This creates a permanent dipole:

$$\overset{\delta+}{\text{H}}-\overset{\delta-}{\text{Br}}$$

The hydrogen atom becomes electron-deficient.

steps.title: Identify the active center
steps.content:
Using the **Identify the Active Electrophilic Center** approach, we look for the specific atom that acts as the electron pair acceptor. 

In the mechanism, the $\\pi$ electrons from the alkene's double bond move toward the hydrogen atom. Therefore, the **active electrophilic center** is the hydrogen atom carrying the partial positive charge ($$H^{\delta+}$$) or the proton ($$H^+$$) that forms during the reaction.

highlights.text: Accept H+ or Hδ+ as the electrophile.

steps.title: Final Identification
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, you can identify the electrophile as either the reagent molecule or the specific active species:

- **Reagent:** $HBr$
- **Active Center:** $H^{\delta+}$ or $H^+$

steps.title: Define "Electrophilic"
steps.content:
The term **electrophilic** refers to the nature of the chemical species that initiates the reaction. An electrophile is an "electron-loving" species (like $H^+$) that is attracted to regions of high electron density.

In alkenes, the carbon-carbon double bond contains a **$\pi$ bond**, which represents a region of high electron density. The reaction is called electrophilic because this $\pi$ bond donates a pair of electrons to the electrophile during the first step of the mechanism.

highlights.text: $\pi$ bond donates electrons to $\text{H}^+$ (or an electrophile)

steps.title: Define "Addition"
steps.content:
The term **addition** describes the change in the structure and stoichiometry of the molecules involved. 

In an addition reaction, **two reactants combine to form a single product**. The double bond in the alkene breaks (converting from unsaturated to saturated), allowing the atoms of the electrophilic reagent to be added across the two carbon atoms. Because there are no byproduct molecules formed, the process is classified as addition.

highlights.text: one product is formed from two reactants

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks in an IB exam, you must address both parts of the definition:

1.  **Electrophilic**: State that the $\pi$ bond of the alkene acts as an electron pair donor to an electrophile.
2.  **Addition**: State that the reaction involves two molecules reacting to produce only one product.

steps.title: Define the electrophilic component
steps.content:
To understand why the reaction is **electrophilic**, we look at the interaction between the reactants. Alkenes contain a $C=C$ double bond, which includes a $\pi$ bond. This $\pi$ bond is a region of high electron density. 

In the mechanism, this electron-rich $\pi$ bond acts as the source of electrons, donating a pair of electrons to an **electrophile** (an electron-deficient species like $H^+$). Because the initial attack is by an electrophile seeking electrons from the $\pi$ bond, the reaction is classified as electrophilic.

highlights.text: State that the $\pi$ bond donates electrons to $\text{H}^+$ (or an electrophile)

steps.title: Define the addition component
steps.content:
The term **addition** describes the overall change in the structure of the molecules. During this process, the double bond of the alkene is broken (becoming saturated) as new atoms are bonded to the carbon atoms.

Since two reactant molecules (the alkene and the electrophilic reagent) combine to form exactly **one single product**, the reaction is classified as an addition reaction.

highlights.text: State that one product is formed from two reactants by electrophilic attack

steps.title: Summary of classification
steps.content:
In summary, the reaction is **electrophilic** because the electron-rich $\pi$ bond donates electrons to an electrophile, and it is an **addition** because two reactants combine to form a single, saturated product. 

This mechanism is a key part of **Topic R3.4: Electron-pair sharing reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Analyze the molecular structure
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we first need to look at the functional group present in the provided structure of ethyl butanoate. This is a key skill covered in **S3.2.2: Functional groups**.

Looking at the center of the molecule, we see a carbon atom double-bonded to one oxygen atom ($C=O$) and single-bonded to another oxygen atom ($-O-$), which is then connected to a second carbon chain ($R'$).

The general structure looks like this:
$$R-C(=O)-O-R'$$

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
The specific arrangement where a carbonyl group ($C=O$) is directly adjacent to an ether linkage ($-O-$) is known as the **ester** functional group, often written as **-COO-**.

In this specific molecule:
- The group on the left ($R$) is a propyl group ($-C_3H_7$).
- The group on the right ($R'$) is an ethyl group ($-C_2H_5$).
- The central link is the ester group.

highlights.text: Identify the homologous series to which ethyl butanoate belongs.

steps.title: Determine the homologous series
steps.content:
Any organic compound containing the $-COO-$ functional group belongs to the homologous series called **esters**. 

You can also recognize this from the name "ethyl butano**ate**". The suffix **-oate** is a standard IUPAC convention for naming members of the ester series.

steps.title: Analyze the IUPAC name
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we examine the IUPAC name provided: **ethyl butanoate**. 

In organic chemistry, names are constructed using specific prefixes and suffixes that correspond to different functional groups. This is a core concept covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Identify the characteristic suffix
steps.content:
Looking at the ending of the name "butanoate", we identify the suffix **-oate**.

According to IUPAC nomenclature rules, the **-oate** suffix is the standard designation for the **ester** homologous series. In these compounds, the name consists of two parts:
1. The alkyl group attached to the oxygen (ethyl).
2. The carboxylate group derived from the parent acid (butanoate).

steps.title: State the homologous series
steps.content:
Since the suffix **-oate** is unique to this functional group, we can conclude that the compound belongs to the **esters**.

Award 1 mark for identifying the correct series as per the markscheme.

parts.content: State the functional group present in ethyl butanoate.

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The question asks us to identify the specific functional group found in the molecule **ethyl butanoate**, based on the provided skeletal structure. In organic chemistry, functional groups are specific atoms or groups of atoms that determine the chemical properties of a molecule.

highlights.text: State the functional group present in ethyl butanoate.

steps.title: Perform structural analysis
steps.content:
Looking at the skeletal structure, we focus on the central part of the molecule where the oxygen atoms are located. We can see a **carbonyl group** ($C=O$) directly bonded to an **oxygen atom** which is then bonded to another carbon chain ($R-COO-R'$).

This specific arrangement, where a carbonyl is adjacent to an ether-like oxygen link, is the defining feature of a specific functional group.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
Based on **Topic S3.2.2: Functional groups**, the structure containing the $-\text{COO}-$ link between two carbon chains is known as an **ester**. 

In this molecule, the 'butanoate' part comes from the four-carbon chain attached to the carbonyl, and the 'ethyl' part comes from the two-carbon chain attached to the single-bonded oxygen.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The functional group present is the **ester** group.

To earn the mark, you can simply state "ester" or show the group formula as $-\text{COO}-$.

steps.title: Analyze the compound name
steps.content: To determine the functional group using nomenclature analysis, we first look closely at the IUPAC name provided in the question: **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Identify the suffix
steps.content:
In IUPAC organic chemistry nomenclature, the suffix (the ending of the name) tells us which functional group is the most important in the molecule. 

Looking at "butanoate", we can see the suffix is **-oate**.

steps.title: Map suffix to group
steps.content:
According to the rules covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, the suffix **-oate** is specifically used for **esters**. 

An ester is formed when a carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol, and its characteristic functional group is the carboxylate group: 

$$-\text{COO}-$$

parts.content: Name the two organic compounds used to prepare ethyl butanoate.

steps.title: Identify the ester
steps.content:
The compound given is **ethyl butanoate**, which belongs to the **ester** functional group. Esters are formed through a condensation reaction (specifically esterification) between an alcohol and a carboxylic acid.

To identify the reactants, we can deconstruct the IUPAC name using nomenclature analysis. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Analyze the alcohol component
steps.content:
In the IUPAC name of an ester, the first word describes the alkyl group derived from the **alcohol**. 

The prefix **ethyl** indicates a two-carbon chain ($C_2$). Therefore, the alcohol used in this reaction is **ethanol**.

steps.title: Analyze the acid component
steps.content:
The second part of the name, ending in **-oate**, describes the part of the molecule derived from the **carboxylic acid**. 

The term **butanoate** stems from "butane," indicating a four-carbon chain ($C_4$). Therefore, the carboxylic acid used is **butanoic acid**.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
Based on our nomenclature analysis, the two organic compounds used to prepare ethyl butanoate are:

1.  **Ethanol** (1 mark)
2.  **Butanoic acid** (1 mark)

steps.title: Identify the ester group
steps.content:
First, we look at the structural formula provided. We can identify the **ester functional group**, which is represented by the $-COO-$ linkage in the middle of the molecule. 

Esters are formed through a condensation reaction between a **carboxylic acid** and an **alcohol**. To find the starting materials, we need to analyze which part of the molecule came from which reactant.

steps.title: Cleave the ester bond
steps.content:
Using the **Structural Analysis** approach, we 'cleave' (cut) the molecule at the specific bond formed during esterification. This bond is always located between the **carbonyl carbon** ($C=O$) and the **single-bonded oxygen** ($-O-$).

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(=\text{O}) \quad \bm{\text{|}} \quad \text{O}\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$$

Breaking the molecule here allows us to see the two precursor 'skeletons'.

steps.title: Identify the acid part
steps.content:
The part of the molecule containing the carbonyl group ($C=O$) comes from the **carboxylic acid**. 

Counting the carbons in this section:
1. Carbon 1 (the carbonyl carbon)
2. Carbon 2 (next $CH_2$)
3. Carbon 3 (next $CH_2$)
4. Carbon 4 (terminal $CH_3$)

A 4-carbon carboxylic acid is called **butanoic acid**. This corresponds to the 'butanoate' part of the name **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Identify the alcohol part
steps.content:
The part of the molecule attached to the single-bonded oxygen comes from the **alcohol**. 

Counting the carbons in this section:
1. Carbon 1 ($CH_2$)
2. Carbon 2 ($CH_3$)

A 2-carbon alcohol is called **ethanol**. This corresponds to the 'ethyl' part of the name **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of the carbon chains on either side of the ester linkage, the two organic compounds used are:

1. **Butanoic acid**
2. **Ethanol**

According to the markscheme, you receive 1 mark for each correct name.

parts.content: Write an equation for the formation of ethyl butanoate from these compounds, including structural formulas.
parts.markscheme:
$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH} + \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOCH_2CH_3} + \mathrm{H_2O}$$
